sections.heading: What Types of Window Damage Can Be Repaired?
sections.content: Not all window damage looks the same, and understanding the type of break helps determine whether repair or full replacement is needed. Our technicians assess every job on-site before recommending a solution.
list: Impact cracks : a starburst or radial pattern spreading from a single point of contact, usually caused by a thrown object, ball, or hail.
Stress cracks : a clean line that starts near the edge and extends inward, often triggered by large temperature swings between indoors and outdoors.
Pressure cracks : a curved crack that can appear in sealed double-pane units when barometric pressure changes cause the glass to flex beyond its limit.
Edge damage : chips or cracks at the perimeter of the glass where it sits in the frame, often caused by settling or improper installation.
Shattered panes : tempered glass breaks into small granular pieces, while annealed glass breaks into large jagged shards. Both require full replacement.

sections.heading: How Does Our Broken Window Repair Process Work?
sections.content: We follow a consistent process on every job to ensure your property is secured quickly and the replacement is installed correctly.
list: Safety first : we clear and clean up all broken glass from the area, inside and out, before any other work begins.
Assess the damage : our technician inspects the frame, seals, and surrounding structure to determine whether a glass-only replacement will work or if the frame also needs attention.
Secure the opening : if permanent glass cannot be installed immediately, we board up or temporarily glaze the opening to protect your property.
Precise measurement : we measure the opening and confirm the glass type, thickness, and any coatings (such as Low-E or tempered) required.
Professional installation : the new glass is set, sealed, and trimmed. We test the window for proper operation and clean the work area before leaving.

sections.heading: Should You Repair or Replace a Broken Window?
sections.content: In most cases involving broken glass, the glass pane itself is replaced while the existing frame is kept. This saves significant cost compared to a full window replacement. Full frame-and-glass replacement is recommended only when the frame is rotted, warped, or structurally compromised. OhMyGlass always assesses whether a glass-only replacement will work before suggesting a full unit replacement. Our repair-first approach typically saves homeowners 60 to 80 percent.

sections.heading: What Should You Do Immediately After a Window Breaks?
sections.content: Taking the right steps in the first few minutes after a window breaks protects your family and makes the repair process smoother.
list: Keep everyone away from the broken glass, especially children and pets. Loose shards can cause serious cuts.
Wear closed-toe shoes if you need to walk near the area.
Do not attempt to remove large pieces of glass still attached to the frame. Leave this to a professional.
Cover the opening with a heavy tarp, cardboard, or plastic sheeting secured with tape to keep weather and debris out.
Take photos of the damage from inside and outside. These help our technician prepare the right materials before arriving.
Call OhMyGlass at 647-803-2730 for immediate assistance. We offer 24/7 emergency response across the GTA.

sections.heading: What Types of Commercial Glass Do We Service?
sections.content: Commercial properties use a variety of glass types, each selected for its performance characteristics. We supply and install all of them.
list: Tempered safety glass : heat-treated to be four to five times stronger than standard annealed glass. When it does break, it shatters into small granular pieces rather than dangerous jagged shards. Required by the Ontario Building Code in many commercial applications.

Laminated safety glass : two or more panes bonded with a polyvinyl butyral (PVB) interlayer. If broken, the fragments adhere to the interlayer rather than falling free, providing security and holding the opening intact.
Insulated glass units (IGUs) : double- or triple-pane assemblies with an air or gas-filled cavity for thermal and acoustic insulation. Common in office buildings for energy code compliance.
Spandrel glass : opaque, coated glass used to conceal structural elements between vision panels on a building facade.
Wired glass : glass with an embedded wire mesh, traditionally used for fire-rated applications in corridors and stairwells.

sections.heading: Ontario Building Code Requirements for Safety Glass
sections.content: The Ontario Building Code (OBC) sets specific requirements for glass in commercial buildings to protect public safety. Under Article 9.6.1.4, certain glazing locations must use tempered, laminated, or wired safety glass conforming to CAN/CGSB-12.1-M or CAN/CGSB-12.11-M standards.
list: Glass sidelights wider than 500 mm at building entrances must be safety glass.
Glass in sliding entrance doors must be tempered, laminated, or wired safety glass.
Entrance door glass that exceeds 0.5 square metres and extends less than 900 mm from the bottom of the door requires safety glass.
Transparent glass panels that could be mistaken for a means of egress must be protected by barriers, railings, or clearly marked.
Glass doors accessible to the public must have hardware, bars, or permanent fixtures that make the door readily apparent.

sections.content: We work with a wide range of commercial and institutional clients across the GTA. Our experience covers the specific glass requirements of different building types and industries.

title: Double Pane Window Replacement Toronto & GTA – Energy Efficient Glass | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Double pane window replacement in Toronto & GTA. Replace just the glass unit, not the frame; save 60-80%. Low-E, argon gas, ENERGY STAR options. Free quote.

pagecontent: Double-pane windows : also called insulated glass units (IGUs) or thermal panes, are the standard for energy-efficient homes and buildings across Ontario. When the sealed unit fails, you lose insulation, clarity, and comfort. OhMyGlass specializes in replacing only the glass unit itself, not the entire window frame, which saves homeowners 60 to 80 percent compared to full window replacement. We serve Toronto and the Greater Toronto Area with a range of IGU options including Low-E coatings and argon gas fills to maximize your home's thermal performance.

sections.heading: How Do Double-Pane Windows Work?
sections.content: A double-pane window consists of two panes of glass separated by a spacer bar, typically aluminum or a warm-edge composite material. The perimeter is hermetically sealed with a primary seal (usually polyisobutylene) and a secondary structural sealant. The cavity between the panes is filled with dry air or an inert gas such as argon or krypton. This gas-filled cavity acts as an insulating barrier, significantly reducing heat transfer through the window compared to a single pane. The spacer bar also contains a desiccant that absorbs any residual moisture inside the unit to prevent internal condensation.

sections.heading: How Do You Know Your Sealed Unit Has Failed?
sections.content: Sealed unit failure is one of the most common window problems in the GTA's climate, where wide temperature swings stress the seals over time. Recognizing the signs early prevents further energy loss.
list: Fog or haze between the panes that cannot be wiped away from either side : this is moisture that has entered through a broken seal.
Visible condensation droplets forming between the panes, especially in the morning or during temperature changes.
White or chalky mineral deposits between the panes : these are left behind as trapped moisture repeatedly evaporates and condenses.
A noticeable increase in outside noise, drafts, or cold spots near the window : the insulating gas has leaked out.
Higher heating or cooling bills without other explanation : failed IGUs can increase energy loss through windows significantly.

sections.heading: What Glass Options Are Available for Replacement IGUs?
sections.content: When we replace your sealed unit, you can choose from several glass and gas options to improve energy performance beyond the original installation.
list: Low-E (low-emissivity) coatings : a microscopically thin metallic coating applied to one surface of the glass that reflects infrared heat. Low-E glass can reduce heat loss through the window by 30 to 50 percent compared to uncoated glass.
Argon gas fill : argon is denser than air and reduces convective heat transfer within the IGU cavity. It improves the window's insulating value by approximately 5 to 15 percent over dry air.
Krypton gas fill : even denser than argon and used primarily in triple-pane units or where narrower cavities are required. It offers higher performance but at a higher cost.

ENERGY STAR compliance : the GTA falls within ENERGY STAR Canada Climate Zone 2 (3,500 to 6,000 heating degree-days). Qualifying windows must meet a U-factor of 1.40 W/m²K or lower, or an Energy Rating of 29 or higher.
Tempered or laminated safety glass : available for IGU replacement where building code or personal safety requires it, such as near doors or at low heights.

sections.heading: How Much Can You Save with IGU-Only Replacement?
sections.content: Replacing just the insulated glass unit while keeping the existing frame is one of the most cost-effective home improvements available. A full window replacement : frame, hardware, and glass : can cost several hundred to over a thousand dollars per window depending on size and type. An IGU-only replacement uses the same frame and trim, which eliminates the cost of new framing materials, interior and exterior finishing, and the labour to remove and reinstall the full unit. Most IGU replacements are completed in under an hour per window with no damage to your walls, trim, or paint. Contact OhMyGlass for a free on-site assessment to confirm whether your frames are in good condition for IGU-only replacement.

title: Foggy Window Repair Toronto & GTA – Restore Clarity | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Foggy window repair in Toronto & GTA. Condensation between panes means a failed seal. We replace the glass unit, not the frame; save 60-80%. Free quote.

pagecontent: Condensation trapped between window panes is one of the most common window problems in Ontario homes, and it signals that your sealed unit has failed. Once the hermetic seal around an insulated glass unit (IGU) is compromised, moisture enters the cavity and causes persistent fog, haze, and mineral deposits that worsen over time. OhMyGlass specializes in foggy window repair across Toronto and the GTA. Our solution replaces only the glass unit : not the entire window frame : making it a fast, cost-effective way to restore clarity, insulation, and curb appeal.

sections.heading: What Causes Windows to Become Foggy?
sections.content: Foggy windows are caused by a failure of the perimeter seal that keeps the insulated glass unit airtight. Several factors contribute to seal degradation over time.
list: Age : most IGU seals have a functional lifespan of 15 to 25 years. As the sealant materials age, they lose flexibility and adhesion.
UV exposure : prolonged direct sunlight breaks down the organic compounds in the sealant, particularly the primary polyisobutylene seal.
Temperature cycling : Ontario's wide seasonal temperature range causes the glass and frame to expand and contract repeatedly, stressing the seal at the glass-to-spacer bond.
Moisture exposure : water that pools on the sill or is driven into the frame by rain can accelerate sealant deterioration from the outside.
Poor original installation : IGUs that were installed without proper glazing support, drainage, or edge clearance may fail prematurely.
Manufacturing defects : occasionally, the factory seal or desiccant is inadequate, leading to early failure within the first few years.

sections.heading: How to Tell If Your Window Seal Has Failed
sections.content: Not every instance of window condensation means a failed seal. Condensation on the interior surface of a window is usually a humidity issue inside the home. Condensation between the panes : visible from neither side and impossible to wipe away : is the definitive sign of seal failure.
list: Persistent haze or fog between the two panes of glass that appears and disappears with temperature changes but never fully clears.
Visible water droplets forming between the panes, especially noticeable in the morning or on cold days.
White or chalky residue between the panes : mineral deposits left behind after moisture repeatedly evaporates inside the cavity.
A milky or cloudy appearance that reduces visibility and blocks natural light.
A distorted or wavy look to objects viewed through the window, caused by moisture interfering with the glass surfaces.

sections.heading: How We Fix Foggy Windows
sections.content: Our repair process replaces the failed insulated glass unit while preserving the existing window frame. This avoids the cost and disruption of a full window replacement.
list: On-site inspection : we confirm the seal failure and check the condition of the frame, sash, and hardware to ensure they can support a new IGU.
Precise measurement : we measure the opening and note the glass type, thickness, spacer width, and any coatings (Low-E, tinted) on the original unit.
IGU fabrication or sourcing : a new factory-sealed unit is built to your exact specifications, including your choice of glass coatings and gas fill.
Careful removal : the interior and exterior glazing stops are removed, and the failed IGU is extracted without damaging the frame or trim.
Installation and sealing : the new unit is set into the frame with proper shimming and glazing, then the stops and sealant are reinstalled. Most windows are completed in under an hour.

sections.heading: Why Replace the Glass Unit Instead of the Whole Window?
sections.content: Full window replacement : removing and replacing the frame, sash, hardware, and glass as a complete unit : makes sense when the frame itself is damaged, rotted, or warped. But in the majority of foggy window cases, the frame is still structurally sound and only the sealed glass unit has failed. Replacing just the IGU typically costs 60 to 80 percent less than a full window replacement. It is also faster (usually under an hour per window), causes no damage to interior or exterior trim, and preserves the original look of your home. OhMyGlass always inspects the frame first and recommends full replacement only when the frame condition warrants it.

sections.heading: When Replacing Just the Glass Is the Better Option
sections.content: Replacing only the damaged glass (the pane or insulated glass unit) saves money and time when the frame is still good. We do not fill or seal cracks; we replace the glass. Our technicians recommend glass-only replacement when the following conditions apply.
list: A single pane or one section of glass is damaged (cracked, chipped, or shattered) and the rest of the window and frame are sound : we replace that glass only.
Foggy or steamy double-pane windows where the seal has failed : we replace only the insulated glass unit (IGU), not the whole frame, for a fraction of the cost of a full window.
Damage is limited to one pane or one IGU so that replacing just that unit restores the window without replacing the entire window.

sections.heading: When Window Glass Replacement Is Necessary
sections.content: Full window glass replacement is the right choice when replacing just the pane or IGU would be unsafe, short-lived, or not cost-effective compared to installing a new window.
list: Cracks longer than about six inches, or chips larger than a quarter, especially in critical viewing areas or near the edge of the glass.
Damage that has penetrated through both layers of a double-pane unit or deep into the pane, compromising the structural strength of the glass.
Multiple chips or cracks in one pane : we replace that pane or the full IGU; we do not patch or fill cracks.
Shattered tempered glass : once tempered glass breaks, the entire pane disintegrates and must be replaced.
Frames or seals that are warped, rotted, or have failed repeatedly : replacement provides better long-term value and safety in these situations.

sections.heading: Cost Comparison: Glass Repair vs Window Glass Replacement
sections.content: Replacing just the glass—a single pane or the insulated glass unit (IGU) while keeping your frame—typically runs around $200 or so depending on size and glass type. We replace the glass; we do not fill or seal cracks. Full window glass replacement (new glass and often frame work) is usually in the $2,000 range per window depending on size, type, and accessibility. OhMyGlass focuses on glass-only replacement when the frame is still good, so you only pay for what you need. We provide a written quote before any work begins so you know your costs upfront.

sections.heading: What Window Types Do We Repair?
sections.content: We repair and replace glass in every residential window style found in GTA homes, from older single-pane windows to modern triple-pane units.
list: Casement windows : hinged at the side and operated by a crank handle. Common in newer homes and condominiums.
Double-hung windows : two sashes that slide vertically, held in place by balance mechanisms. The most common window type in Ontario homes.
Single-hung windows : similar to double-hung but only the bottom sash moves.
Sliding windows : sashes that slide horizontally along a track. Common in basements and bedrooms.
Awning windows : hinged at the top and open outward. Often installed above or below other windows for ventilation.
Bay and bow windows : a group of windows that project outward from the wall, creating additional interior space.
Picture windows : large fixed (non-opening) windows designed to maximize natural light and views.
Basement and hopper windows : smaller windows hinged at the bottom, opening inward. Found in basements and bathrooms.

sections.heading: Common Residential Window Problems
sections.content: Most residential window issues fall into one of a few categories. Many of them can be solved by replacing the glass or sealed unit alone, without touching the frame.
list: Cracked or broken glass : from accidental impacts, thermal stress, or pressure changes. A single cracked pane can be replaced without disturbing the rest of the window.
Foggy or hazy insulated glass : caused by seal failure in the double- or triple-pane unit. Moisture enters the cavity and causes persistent condensation between the panes.
Drafts and air leaks : worn weatherstripping or failed seals allow air infiltration around the sash or between the glass and frame.
Difficult operation : cranks that are stripped, balances that have failed, or tracks that are dirty or damaged can prevent windows from opening or closing properly.
Condensation on interior glass surface : while not a glass defect, this indicates high indoor humidity or insufficient insulation and may be improved with a Low-E glass upgrade.

sections.heading: How Our Residential Repair Process Works
sections.content: We follow a consistent process on every residential job to deliver reliable results with minimal disruption to your home.
list: In-home assessment : we inspect the window, identify the problem, and check the condition of the frame, sash, and hardware.
Repair recommendation : we explain whether a glass-only replacement, an IGU swap, a hardware fix, or a full window replacement is the right solution, and why.
Precise measurement : if glass replacement is needed, we take exact measurements including glass type, thickness, coatings, and spacer configuration.
Professional installation : we remove the damaged glass carefully, clean the frame, install the new glass or IGU, and reseal everything. Interior and exterior stops are reinstalled.
Final check and cleanup : we verify proper fit, test the window's operation, and clean up all debris before leaving.

sections.heading: How New Glass Improves Energy Efficiency
sections.content: Upgrading your window glass can meaningfully reduce your home's heating and cooling costs. In Ontario's climate, windows are one of the largest sources of heat loss in a home. Low-E coated glass reflects infrared heat back into the room during winter and blocks solar heat gain in summer, reducing heat transfer through the glass by 30 to 50 percent compared to standard clear glass. Argon gas fill in the IGU cavity further reduces convective heat loss. Together, these upgrades can qualify your windows for ENERGY STAR certification under Canada's Climate Zone 2, which applies to the Greater Toronto Area. OhMyGlass can recommend the right combination of glass options for your home's orientation and heating needs.

sections.heading: What Types of Storefront Glass Do We Install?
sections.content: Storefront glass must meet specific performance requirements for safety, energy efficiency, and appearance. We supply and install all common types.
list: Tempered safety glass : the standard for most storefront applications. Heat-treated to be four to five times stronger than annealed glass and breaks into small, relatively harmless pieces if shattered.

Insulated glass units (IGUs) : double-pane assemblies with an insulating gas cavity, used where energy performance and interior comfort are priorities.
Tinted glass : reduces solar heat gain and glare while maintaining visibility. Available in bronze, grey, green, and blue tones.
Low-iron glass : an ultra-clear glass with reduced green tint, used for high-end retail displays where true colour rendering of merchandise is important.

sections.heading: Safety Glass Requirements Under Ontario Building Code
sections.content: The Ontario Building Code mandates safety glass in several storefront and commercial entrance applications to protect the public. OhMyGlass ensures every commercial installation complies with these requirements. Under OBC Article 9.6.1.4, glass in commercial entrance doors, sidelights wider than 500 mm, and sliding doors at public entrances must be tempered, laminated, or wired safety glass conforming to CAN/CGSB standards. Additionally, transparent glass panels that could be mistaken for a walkway or exit must be marked or protected by barriers.

seo.meta_description: Aluminum storefront glass repair and installation in Toronto & GTA. Narrow, medium, and wide stile systems. Thermally broken options. Fast, professional service.

pagecontent: Aluminum storefront systems are the backbone of commercial glass facades across the Greater Toronto Area. They combine structural strength, clean sightlines, and weather resistance in a lightweight, low-maintenance package. OhMyGlass repairs and installs aluminum storefront glass for businesses of all sizes : from a single replacement pane in an existing frame to a complete new storefront system. We work with all major framing profiles including narrow stile, medium stile, and wide stile configurations, and offer both thermally broken and non-thermally broken options to suit your building's energy and budget requirements.

sections.heading: Types of Aluminum Storefront Systems
sections.content: Aluminum storefront framing comes in several standard profiles, each designed for different traffic levels and hardware requirements.
list: Narrow stile : vertical stiles of 1-3/4 to 2-1/8 inches. Designed for light to moderate traffic locations such as office lobbies and strip mall entrances. Provides maximum glass area and minimal frame visibility.
Medium stile : vertical stiles of 3-1/2 inches. Suited for moderate to heavy traffic in retail stores, restaurants, and medical offices. Balances glass visibility with structural strength.

Wide stile : vertical stiles of 5 inches. Built for heavy traffic in public buildings, schools, and transit facilities. Accommodates panic hardware, closers, and a wider variety of operating hardware.

Thermally broken systems : framing that includes an insulating barrier (typically a polyamide strip) between the interior and exterior aluminum to reduce heat transfer through the frame. Required in many new builds for energy code compliance.
Non-thermally broken systems : standard aluminum framing without an insulating break. Common in older buildings and non-conditioned spaces.

sections.heading: Glass Options for Aluminum Storefronts
sections.content: Aluminum storefront frames accommodate a wide range of glass types and thicknesses, from single panes to insulated glass units.
list: Standard clear tempered glass : the most common choice for commercial storefronts, meeting Ontario Building Code safety requirements.
Laminated safety glass : provides enhanced security and holds together when broken, making forced entry more difficult.
Insulated glass units (IGUs) : double-pane units with an air or argon cavity for improved thermal and acoustic performance.
Tinted and Low-E coated glass : reduces solar heat gain, glare, and UV transmission while maintaining outward visibility.
Spandrel glass : opaque, coated glass used between vision panels to conceal floor slabs, structural members, and mechanical systems.

sections.heading: Our Installation and Repair Process
sections.content: Whether you need a single pane replaced in an existing aluminum frame or a complete new storefront system installed, our process is consistent and professional.
list: Site survey : we measure the opening, identify the existing framing profile (manufacturer, series, finish), and note any code requirements.
Material sourcing : glass is fabricated or sourced to the exact size and specification. For frame repairs, we match the existing aluminum profile and finish.
Glazing : glass is dry-glazed into the frame using EPDM gaskets on both interior and exterior faces, providing a weather-tight seal without wet sealants.
Hardware and finishing : door hardware, closers, locks, and threshold components are installed or replaced as needed.
Final inspection : we verify the seal, alignment, and operation of all doors and panels before completing the job.

sections.heading: Why Choose Aluminum Framing for Your Storefront?
sections.content: Aluminum is the dominant framing material for commercial storefronts for several practical reasons.
list: Durability : extruded 6063-T5 aluminum alloy resists corrosion, warping, and rot, even in Ontario's freeze-thaw climate.
Lightweight : aluminum weighs about one-third as much as steel, reducing structural load on the building and simplifying installation.
Low maintenance : anodized or powder-coated finishes hold up for decades without painting or staining.
Design flexibility : aluminum extrusions can be manufactured in a wide range of profiles, finishes, and colours to match any architectural style.
Recyclability : aluminum is one of the most recyclable building materials, with no loss of quality when reprocessed.

sections.heading: Common Door Glass Problems
sections.content: Door glass is subject to many of the same issues as window glass, plus additional stresses from daily opening and closing.
list: Cracked or broken glass : from impacts, forced entry attempts, or thermal stress. Door glass is required by building code to be tempered or laminated safety glass.
Foggy insulated glass : the sealed unit in a patio door or insulated entry door has failed, causing condensation between the panes.
Failed seals and drafts : worn weatherstripping or deteriorated glazing seals around the glass panel allow air and water infiltration.
Difficult sliding : patio and sliding door tracks can accumulate debris, or the rollers and track can wear out, causing the door to drag or stick.
Broken or worn hardware : locks, handles, and hinges can fail with daily use, compromising security and ease of operation.

sections.heading: Glass Options for Door Replacement
sections.content: When replacing door glass, the Ontario Building Code requires safety glass in all door applications. We offer several options depending on your priorities.
list: Tempered safety glass : the standard for door glass. If broken, it shatters into small granular pieces rather than dangerous shards.
Laminated safety glass : two panes bonded with a PVB interlayer. Provides enhanced security because the glass holds together even when broken, making forced entry more difficult.
Low-E insulated glass units : double-pane units with a Low-E coating and argon gas fill for improved energy efficiency. Ideal for patio doors and entry doors exposed to weather.
Decorative glass : frosted, textured, leaded, or stained glass options for entry doors and sidelights where privacy or aesthetics are important.
Obscure or privacy glass : diffused or patterned glass that lets light through while blocking visibility, suitable for bathroom doors and side panels.

sections.heading: Our Door Glass Repair Process

sections.heading: What Types of Mirrors Do We Install?

Beveled mirrors : feature an angled, polished edge that catches light and adds visual depth. Available with bevel widths from 1/2 inch to 1 inch.

Full-length mirrors : floor-to-ceiling or large-format mirrors for dressing rooms, closets, hallways, and retail fitting areas.
Antiqued and decorative mirrors : mirrors with an aged, distressed, or tinted finish for decorative and design-focused applications.
Safety-backed mirrors : mirrors with an adhesive safety film on the back that holds fragments together if the mirror breaks. Required in many commercial and public installations.

sections.heading: Edge Finish and Glass Thickness Options
sections.content: The edge finish and thickness of a mirror affect both its appearance and its suitability for the installation method.
list: Flat polished edge : the edge is ground and polished to a smooth, sleek finish. The most popular choice for frameless installations and modern interiors.
Beveled edge : the edge is cut at an angle (typically 1/2 to 1 inch wide) and polished, creating a subtle frame effect that adds elegance without an actual frame.
Seamed edge : the edge is lightly sanded to remove sharpness but not polished. Used when the mirror will be set in a frame or channel that covers the edge.
Pencil polished edge : the edge is rounded and polished into a smooth curved profile. Common on smaller mirrors and decorative pieces.
Standard thickness is 1/4 inch (6 mm), which works for most mirrors up to about 4 by 6 feet. For larger mirrors, 3/8 inch glass is recommended to prevent bowing and distortion.

sections.heading: How We Install Custom Mirrors
sections.content: Proper installation is essential for safety and longevity, especially with large or heavy mirrors. Our installation method depends on the mirror's size, weight, and the wall structure.
list: French cleat (Z-bar) mounting : the industry standard for large, heavy mirrors. A metal bar is secured to the wall studs, and a matching bar is attached to the back of the mirror. The mirror hangs on the cleat, distributing weight evenly across the wall.
J-channel bottom support : a metal channel is mounted at the base to support the mirror's weight, with clips or adhesive at the top to hold it in position. Common for bathroom vanity mirrors.

Adhesive mounting : neutral-cure mirror-safe silicone and double-sided glazing tape are used for lighter mirrors on smooth, flat surfaces. The adhesive must be compatible with the mirror's reflective coating to prevent desilvering.
Mechanical clips : small metal or plastic clips mounted to the wall hold the mirror at its edges. A simple, economical method for standard-size mirrors.

sections.content: We install mirrors in a wide range of settings across the GTA.

title: Window & Door Hardware Repairs Toronto & GTA | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Window and door hardware repair in Toronto & GTA. Cranks, hinges, locks, balances, weatherstripping, rollers. Restore function without full replacement. Free quote.

pagecontent: A window or door that will not open, close, lock, or seal properly is more than an annoyance : it is a security risk and a source of energy loss. OhMyGlass repairs and replaces window and door hardware across the Greater Toronto Area. From a stripped window crank to a broken patio door roller, our technicians diagnose the problem on-site and carry common replacement parts to complete most repairs in a single visit. Hardware repair is often far less expensive than replacing the entire window or door, and it can restore full functionality to units that are otherwise in good condition.

sections.heading: What Hardware Do We Repair and Replace?
sections.content: We work with all types of window and door operating hardware found in residential and commercial buildings.
list: Crank handles and operators : the gear-driven mechanism that opens and closes casement and awning windows. When the gears strip, the handle spins freely without moving the sash.
Hinges : friction hinges on casement windows and butt hinges on doors. Worn or corroded hinges cause sticking, sagging, and difficulty closing.
Locks and latches : sash locks on double-hung windows, multi-point locking systems on doors, deadbolts, and commercial door locks. A broken lock compromises security immediately.

Window balances : the spring-loaded mechanisms (block-and-tackle or spiral) inside the jambs of double-hung windows that hold the sash at any position. When a balance fails, the sash will not stay open or slides down on its own.

Weatherstripping : the flexible seals around sashes, meeting rails, and door frames that block drafts, rain, and dust. Worn weatherstripping is one of the most common causes of energy loss around windows and doors.
Rollers and tracks : the wheels and channels that allow sliding windows and patio doors to glide smoothly. Worn rollers or damaged tracks cause dragging, sticking, and difficulty locking.
Handles and pulls : door handles, window lift handles, and pull hardware that wear out or break with daily use.

sections.heading: Common Hardware Problems and Solutions
sections.content: Most hardware issues have straightforward solutions that our technicians can resolve on-site.
list: Stripped crank : the gears inside the operator are worn. Solution: replace the operator mechanism and crank handle as a unit.
Stiff or stuck hinge : dirt, paint, or corrosion is preventing smooth movement. Solution: clean, lubricate, and adjust, or replace the hinge if it is bent or corroded beyond repair.
Broken lock : the latch or cam mechanism has failed. Solution: replace the lock hardware with a compatible unit and verify the keeper alignment.
Sash that will not stay up : the balance spring has lost tension or the cord has broken. Solution: replace the balance mechanism in the jamb channel.
Drafty window or door : weatherstripping has compressed, cracked, or fallen out. Solution: remove the old material and install new weatherstripping matched to the profile.
Patio door that drags : rollers are worn, flat, or the track is bent. Solution: replace the roller assemblies and straighten or replace the track.

sections.heading: How to Maintain Your Window and Door Hardware
sections.content: Regular maintenance extends the life of your hardware and prevents most common failures before they start.
list: Clean tracks and channels at least twice a year with mild soap and water to prevent debris buildup that causes sticking and wear.

Lubricate moving parts : hinges, operators, locks, and rollers : with a silicone-based spray. Avoid petroleum-based lubricants like WD-40 for long-term use, as they can attract dust and gum up over time.
Inspect weatherstripping annually. Look for cracks, gaps, compression, or sections that have pulled away from the frame. Replace as needed.
Tighten loose screws on hinges, handles, and lock hardware at least once a year. Loose screws accelerate wear on the surrounding material.
Check window balances by opening a double-hung window halfway and releasing it. If it slides down on its own, the balance needs attention.
Test all locks seasonally to ensure they engage fully and the keeper is aligned. A lock that does not fully engage provides no security.

sections.heading: When Should You Repair vs Replace the Entire Window or Door?
sections.content: Hardware repair makes sense when the glass and frame are in good condition and only the operating mechanism has failed. A crank replacement, hinge swap, or new set of balances costs a fraction of a full window replacement and restores the unit to full functionality. Replacement of the entire window or door is more appropriate when the frame is rotted, warped, or structurally unsound, when multiple components have failed simultaneously, or when the unit is so old that replacement parts are no longer available. OhMyGlass evaluates both options on every job and recommends the most cost-effective solution.

seo.meta_description: Window glass replacement in Toronto & GTA. Single-pane, double-pane IGU, tempered, Low-E. Fast service, free quote. Save 60-80% vs full window replacement.

sections.heading: What Types of Replacement Glass Do We Offer?
sections.content: We supply and install the full range of window glass types used in Ontario homes and commercial buildings.
list: Annealed (float) glass : standard clear glass used in many older and non-safety applications. The most economical option for basic single-pane replacement.

Tempered safety glass : heat-treated to be four to five times stronger than annealed glass. Required by the Ontario Building Code in locations near doors, at low heights, and in other high-risk areas.
Laminated glass : two panes bonded with a PVB interlayer for safety and security. Holds together when broken, providing protection against forced entry and flying debris.
Low-E coated glass : features a microscopically thin metallic coating that reflects infrared heat, reducing heat transfer by 30 to 50 percent compared to uncoated glass.
Tinted glass : bronze, grey, green, or blue tones that reduce glare and solar heat gain while maintaining visibility.
Obscure and frosted glass : textured or etched glass that allows light transmission while blocking clear visibility, suitable for bathrooms and privacy applications.
Insulated glass units (IGUs) : factory-sealed double- or triple-pane assemblies with an air or gas-filled cavity for thermal and acoustic insulation.

sections.heading: How Does Window Glass Replacement Work?
sections.content: Our process is designed for accuracy and efficiency, with most single-window replacements completed in 30 to 60 minutes.
list: On-site measurement : we measure the exact dimensions of the opening and note the glass type, thickness, coatings, and any spacer configuration for IGUs.
Glass fabrication or sourcing : the replacement glass or IGU is fabricated to your exact specifications. Tempered glass must be cut to size before heat treatment, so precise measurement is critical.
Removal of old glass : glazing stops (the trim that holds the glass in the frame) are carefully removed, and the old glass is extracted without damaging the frame or surrounding finish.
Frame preparation : the frame channel is cleaned, old sealant is removed, and the frame is inspected for any damage that could affect the new installation.
Installation : the new glass is set into the frame with proper shimming to ensure even weight distribution. Glazing gaskets or sealant are applied, and the stops are reinstalled.
Quality check and cleanup : we inspect the seal, verify the window operates correctly, clean the new glass, and remove all debris from the work area.

sections.heading: Energy-Efficient Glass Options for Ontario Homes
sections.content: Ontario's climate : with cold winters and warm, humid summers : makes window energy performance especially important. Upgrading your window glass during a replacement is one of the most effective ways to reduce heating and cooling costs.
list: Low-E coatings reflect infrared heat back into your home in winter and block solar heat gain in summer. A Low-E IGU can reduce heat loss through the window by 30 to 50 percent.
Argon gas fill in the IGU cavity is denser than air and reduces convective heat transfer, improving insulating performance by approximately 5 to 15 percent.
Triple-pane IGUs add a third pane and second insulating cavity, offering up to 50 percent better performance than a standard double-pane unit.
The Greater Toronto Area falls within ENERGY STAR Canada Climate Zone 2. Windows qualifying for this zone must achieve a U-factor of 1.40 W/m²K or lower, or an Energy Rating of 29 or higher.

Warm-edge spacers : composite or stainless steel spacers between the panes reduce heat transfer at the edge of the IGU compared to traditional aluminum spacers, reducing condensation on the interior glass near the frame.

sections.heading: What Factors Affect the Cost of Glass Replacement?
sections.content: The cost of window glass replacement varies depending on several factors. We provide a free, detailed quote before any work begins so you know exactly what to expect.
list: Glass type : tempered and laminated glass cost more than standard annealed glass due to additional processing. Low-E coatings and gas fills add to the cost of an IGU.
Size and thickness : larger panes and thicker glass require more material and careful handling.
Number of panes : a single-pane replacement is less expensive than a double- or triple-pane IGU replacement.
Accessibility : windows on upper floors, behind landscaping, or in tight spaces may require additional time or equipment.
Quantity : replacing glass in multiple windows at the same time is more cost-effective per window than individual replacements.
Urgency : after-hours and emergency service is available 24/7 but may carry a premium compared to scheduled appointments.

title: How to Tell If Your Window Seal Is Broken (And What to Do) | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Learn the signs of a broken window seal: fog between panes, drafts, higher bills. What to do next and when to replace the glass unit. Free quote in the GTA.

pagecontent: A broken window seal is one of the most common causes of foggy, hazy, or drafty windows in Toronto and the GTA. The seal runs around the edge of double- and triple-pane insulated glass units (IGUs), keeping moisture out and the insulating gas in. When it fails, you lose clarity, efficiency, and comfort. Here’s how to tell if your window seal is broken and what to do about it.

sections.heading: What Are the Signs of a Broken Window Seal?
sections.content: Seal failure is usually easy to spot once you know what to look for.
list: Fog or condensation between the panes that you cannot wipe away from either side. This is the most common sign: moisture has entered the airspace because the seal has failed.
A white, hazy, or cloudy film or mineral deposits between the panes, often worse in the morning or when the temperature changes.
Drafts or cold spots near the window even when it is closed, or a noticeable increase in outside noise.
Higher heating or cooling bills with no other obvious cause, as the insulating gas has escaped and the window no longer performs as designed.

sections.heading: Why Do Window Seals Fail?
sections.content: Ontario’s wide temperature swings and strong sun put constant stress on the seal. Over time, the sealant can crack, shrink, or pull away from the glass. Older windows are more likely to fail, but even newer units can fail if there was a manufacturing defect or damage during installation. Once the seal is broken, the condition cannot be reversed; the only fix is to replace the insulated glass unit.

sections.heading: What Should You Do If the Seal Is Broken?
sections.content: Replace the glass unit, not the whole window. OhMyGlass replaces the IGU while keeping your existing frame and trim, which typically saves 60 to 80 percent compared to a full window replacement. We measure on-site, order or fabricate the correct sealed unit (including Low-E and argon options), and install it with minimal disruption. For a free assessment and quote anywhere in the GTA, contact OhMyGlass or request a free quote online.

title: Foggy Double-Pane Windows: Repair vs Replace | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Foggy double-pane windows mean the seal has failed. Repair by replacing the glass unit only; save 60-80% vs full window replacement. Free quote in Toronto & GTA.

pagecontent: Foggy or cloudy double-pane windows are a clear sign that the sealed insulating glass unit (IGU) has failed. Moisture has gotten between the panes and cannot be wiped away. The good news: you usually do not need to replace the entire window. Replacing just the glass unit restores clarity and efficiency at a fraction of the cost.

sections.heading: Why Do Double-Pane Windows Get Foggy?
sections.content: Double-pane windows have two layers of glass with a sealed air or gas space between them. A perimeter seal keeps that space dry and often filled with argon gas. When the seal fails—due to age, temperature stress, or manufacturing defect—moisture enters the space. That moisture condenses on the glass, causing the fog or haze you see. The condition is permanent; the only fix is to replace the IGU.

sections.heading: Repair (IGU Replacement) vs Full Window Replacement
sections.content: In most cases, the frame and sash are still in good shape. Replacing only the glass unit—the IGU—preserves the frame, trim, and look of your window while restoring clarity and insulation. IGU-only replacement typically costs 60 to 80 percent less than a full window replacement and can often be completed in under an hour per window. Full replacement is only necessary when the frame is damaged, rotted, or you want a different style or material.

sections.heading: What to Expect When You Replace the Glass Unit
sections.content: OhMyGlass measures the opening on-site, orders or fabricates the correct sealed unit (including Low-E and argon options for better energy performance), and installs it with minimal disruption. pagecontent: When a window or door glass breaks—from a break-in, storm, or accident—you need a fast, reliable response. OhMyGlass provides 24/7 emergency glass repair across Toronto and the GTA. Here’s what to expect when you call.

sections.heading: When You Call
sections.content: Our team will ask about the type and size of the break, whether anyone is injured, and whether the opening is secure. This helps us send the right crew with the right materials. We aim to have a technician on-site within 30 to 60 minutes depending on your location in the GTA.

sections.heading: On-Site: Securing the Opening
sections.content: The technician will clean up broken glass safely, then secure the opening with a board-up (reinforced plywood or polycarbonate) or temporary glazing. Your property is protected from weather and intrusion. We also take precise measurements so the permanent replacement glass can be fabricated or sourced.

sections.heading: Permanent Replacement
sections.content: Once the permanent glass is ready, we schedule a return visit at a time that works for you to complete the installation. For emergencies in Toronto, Mississauga, Newmarket, Vaughan, Scarborough, North York, Etobicoke, Markham, Richmond Hill, or anywhere in the GTA, call OhMyGlass at 647-803-2730 or request a callback. We are available 24/7, including holidays.

title: How Much Does Window Glass Replacement Cost in the GTA? | OhMyGlass

keywords: how much does window glass cost
seo.meta_description: Window glass replacement cost in the GTA: factors that affect price, typical ranges, and how to get a free quote. Save 60-80% vs full window replacement.

pagecontent: The cost of window glass replacement in the Greater Toronto Area depends on the size of the pane, the type of glass, and whether you are replacing a single pane or an entire insulated glass unit (IGU). In most cases, replacing only the glass—not the whole window—saves 60 to 80 percent compared to a full window replacement.

sections.heading: What Affects the Cost?
sections.content: Glass type (single-pane, double-pane IGU, tempered, laminated, Low-E), size and thickness, and accessibility (e.g. upper floors, difficult access) all affect the final price. Quantity matters too: replacing several windows at once is often more cost-effective per window than a single pane.

sections.heading: Typical Ranges
sections.content: Single-pane replacement is often the least expensive. IGU (double-pane unit) replacement costs more but is still far less than a full window. Tempered or safety glass and upgrades like Low-E or argon add to the cost. OhMyGlass provides a free, written quote before any work begins so you know exactly what to expect.

sections.heading: Get a Free Quote
sections.content: We serve the entire GTA with free on-site or photo-based quotes. Describe your project online, call 647-803-2730, or use our free quote form. We respond quickly and recommend the most cost-effective option—repair or replace—for your situation.

title: Storefront Glass Repair: What Toronto Business Owners Need to Know | OhMyGlass

seo.meta_description: Storefront glass repair for Toronto businesses: types of damage, code requirements, and fast service to minimize downtime. Free quote, 24/7 emergency.

pagecontent: For Toronto business owners, a damaged storefront is a security risk, a curb-appeal problem, and a potential loss of sales. OhMyGlass provides storefront glass repair and replacement across the GTA, with emergency board-up and flexible scheduling to minimize disruption.

sections.heading: Common Storefront Glass Issues
sections.content: Break-ins and vandalism, impact damage, seal failure in insulated units, and worn or damaged frames and gaskets. We handle all of these: board-up and secure the opening first if needed, then replace with code-compliant safety glass (tempered or laminated) as required by the Ontario Building Code.

sections.heading: What Toronto Business Owners Should Know
sections.content: Entrance doors and sidelights, and large storefront panels in certain locations, must use safety glass. We supply and install the correct type and can work after hours or on weekends to fit your schedule. Emergency response is available 24/7 so your property is secured as quickly as possible.
